sub_tasks.requirements: Method and code fidelity to the paper

The code implements the residual building block y = F(x, {W_i}) + x (Equation 1) with parameter-free identity shortcuts for same-dimension connections, and y = F(x, {W_i}) + W_s*x (Equation 2) with W_s implemented as 1×1 convolutions with stride 2 for dimension-matching projection shortcuts, as described in Section 3.1.
sub_tasks.task_category: Method and code fidelity to the paper

sub_tasks.requirements: The bottleneck building block for ResNet-50, ResNet-101, and ResNet-152 is implemented as a stack of three layers (1×1 conv, 3×3 conv, 1×1 conv) where the 1×1 layers reduce and then restore dimensions, with parameter-free identity shortcuts for non-dimension-changing connections and option B projection shortcuts (1×1 conv) used only for increasing dimensions, as described in Section 3.4 and Table 1.
sub_tasks.task_category: Method and code fidelity to the paper

sub_tasks.requirements: Batch normalization (BN) is applied right after each convolution and before activation throughout all plain and residual network models, with He et al. [13] weight initialization and no dropout used at any point, as specified in Section 3.3.
sub_tasks.task_category: Method and code fidelity to the paper

sub_tasks.requirements: Shortcut option A (zero-padding for increasing dimensions, zero extra parameters) and option B (1×1 projection convolutions with stride 2 for increasing dimensions only, identity shortcuts otherwise) are both correctly implemented as distinct configurations; the plain network baselines use 3×3 filters, stride-2 downsampling convolutions, and end with a global average pooling layer followed by a 1000-way fully-connected softmax layer for ImageNet, as described in Sections 3.2 and 3.3.
sub_tasks.task_category: Method and code fidelity to the paper

sub_tasks.requirements: The CIFAR-10 architecture follows the 6n+2 stacked weighted layer formula with 3×3 convolutions on feature maps of sizes {32×32, 16×16, 8×8}, filter counts {16, 32, 64} respectively, 2n layers per map size, 3n total identity shortcuts (option A in all cases), subsampling via stride-2 convolutions, and ending with global average pooling, a 10-way fully-connected layer, and softmax, as specified in Section 4.2.
sub_tasks.task_category: Method and code fidelity to the paper

sub_tasks.requirements: Data and preprocessing fidelity

ImageNet training preprocessing applies scale augmentation by randomly sampling the shorter side in [256, 480], takes a 224×224 random crop from the image or its horizontal flip, subtracts the per-pixel mean, and applies the standard color augmentation from Krizhevsky et al. [21], as described in Section 3.3.

CIFAR-10 training augmentation pads 4 pixels on each side of each 32×32 image and randomly samples a 32×32 crop from the padded image or its horizontal flip; test evaluation uses only a single view of the original 32×32 image without any augmentation, as specified in Section 4.2.

ImageNet comparison-study evaluation uses standard 10-crop testing; best-result evaluation uses the fully-convolutional form with scores averaged over multiple scales where the shorter side is in {224, 256, 384, 480, 640}, as described in Section 3.3.
Experiment execution and reproducibility

ImageNet training uses SGD with mini-batch size 256, initial learning rate 0.1 divided by 10 when the error plateaus, training for up to 60×10^4 iterations, weight decay 0.0001, momentum 0.9, He et al. [13] initialization, BN after each conv and before activation, and no dropout, as specified in Section 3.3.

CIFAR-10 training uses mini-batch size 128 on two GPUs, initial learning rate 0.1 divided by 10 at 32k and 48k iterations, training terminated at 64k iterations, a 45k/5k train/validation split used to determine the schedule, weight decay 0.0001, momentum 0.9, He et al. [13] initialization, BN, and no dropout, as specified in Section 4.2.

ResNet-110 (n=18) training uses a warm-up phase with learning rate 0.01 until training error falls below 80% (approximately 400 iterations), after which the learning rate returns to 0.1 and the standard CIFAR-10 schedule (divide by 10 at 32k and 48k, stop at 64k) continues; ResNet-110 is run 5 independent times and reported as 'best (mean ± std)', as described in Section 4.2.
Evaluation protocol and metric correctness

ImageNet evaluation reports both top-1 and top-5 error rates (%) using 10-crop testing on the 50k ImageNet 2012 validation set (1000-class, 1.28M training images) for comparison studies, and reports final ensemble results on the 100k test images as submitted to the ILSVRC test server, as described in Section 3.3.

CIFAR-10 evaluation reports classification error (%) on the 10k test images using a single view of the original 32×32 image; for ResNet-110 specifically, the result must be reported as 'best (mean ± std)' across 5 independent runs matching the protocol of Srivastava et al. [43], as specified in Section 4.2 and Table 6.

MS COCO detection evaluation reports both the PASCAL VOC metric (mAP@IoU=0.5) and the standard COCO metric (mAP@IoU=[.5:.05:.95]) on the 40k validation set, and reports final single-model and 3-model ensemble results on the 20k test-dev set submitted to the evaluation server, as described in the detection appendix.
Result match versus the paper's reported targets

On the ImageNet 2012 validation set with 10-crop testing (Table 2), the 34-layer ResNet reduces top-1 error by 3.5% compared to the plain-34 network, and the 34-layer ResNet outperforms the 18-layer ResNet by 2.8%, as reported in Section 3.3.
sub_tasks.task_category: Result match versus the paper's reported targets

sub_tasks.requirements: ResNet-152 achieves a single-model top-5 validation error of 4.49% on ImageNet; an ensemble of 6 ResNet models (including two 152-layer models) achieves 3.57% top-5 error on the ImageNet test set, winning 1st place in ILSVRC 2015 classification, as reported in Section 3.4 and Table 5.
sub_tasks.task_category: Result match versus the paper's reported targets

sub_tasks.requirements: On the CIFAR-10 test set (Table 6), ResNet-110 (n=18, 6×18+2=110 layers) achieves a best test error of 6.43%, and ResNet-1202 (n=200) achieves a test error of 7.93%, as reported in Section 4.2.
sub_tasks.task_category: Result match versus the paper's reported targets

sub_tasks.requirements: On MS COCO validation (Table 8), replacing VGG-16 with ResNet-101 in Faster R-CNN yields a 6.0% absolute increase in mAP@[.5:.05:.95], constituting a 28% relative improvement, with the gain attributed solely to the learned representations of the deeper network, as reported in Section 5.
sub_tasks.task_category: Result match versus the paper's reported targets

sub_tasks.requirements: Artifact completeness and provenance

The repository contains source code implementing all six ImageNet ResNet variants (ResNet-18, ResNet-34, ResNet-50, ResNet-101, ResNet-152) and the plain-18 and plain-34 baselines, with architectures matching Table 1 including the 3.8B FLOPs ResNet-50, the 11.3B FLOPs ResNet-152, and the corresponding plain counterparts referenced in Section 3.2.

The repository contains CIFAR-10 experiment code for all reported depth configurations n∈{3, 5, 7, 9, 18, 200} yielding ResNet-20/32/44/56/110/1202, along with training logs or checkpoint files and a metrics file recording final test errors reproducible against Table 6 values, as reported in Section 4.2.
